sections.title: Purpose of Building Regulations
sections.summary: Establishes the purpose of building regulations in San Diego to safeguard public health, safety, and general welfare by regulating the design, construction, quality of materials, use and occupancy, location, and maintenance of all buildings and structures.
key_points: All construction must comply with adopted building codes
The Building Official has authority to interpret and enforce building regulations
Permits required before commencing construction work

sections.title: When Building Permits Are Required
sections.summary: A building permit is required for the construction, erection, alteration, moving, demolition, repair, or conversion of any building or structure regulated by the San Diego Municipal Code.
key_points: Any person who constructs, erects, alters, moves, demolishes, repairs, or converts any building must first obtain a permit
Permits must be obtained before work begins
Applies to both residential and commercial properties
Includes changes in use or occupancy classification

sections.title: Permit Exemptions
sections.summary: Lists work that is exempt from building permit requirements. Note that exemption from permits does not authorize work in violation of building codes.
key_points: One-story detached accessory structures used as tool/storage sheds, playhouses, and similar uses, provided the floor area does not exceed 120 square feet
Fences not over 6 feet high (3 feet in required front yard setback)
Retaining walls not over 3 feet high measured from bottom of footing to top of wall, unless supporting a surcharge
Painting, papering, tiling, carpeting, countertops, and similar finish work
Temporary motion picture, television, and theater stage sets and scenery
Prefabricated swimming pools that are less than 24 inches deep
Shade cloth structures for nurseries and agriculture
Swings and other playground equipment
Window awnings not projecting more than 54 inches
Non-fixed and movable fixtures, cases, racks, counters, and partitions not over 5 feet 9 inches high
Decks and platforms not more than 30 inches above adjacent grade and not over any basement or story below
Replacement of existing equipment with similar equipment (like-for-like HVAC, water heater if same type/location)
Minor electrical work: replacing switches, receptacles, and lighting fixtures
Minor plumbing work: replacing faucets, valves, and similar devices

sections.title: Accessory Dwelling Units
sections.summary: Regulations for Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs) and Junior ADUs (JADUs) in San Diego, implementing California Government Code Section 65852.2.
key_points: One ADU and one JADU permitted on lots with existing or proposed single-family dwelling
Multiple ADUs permitted on lots with existing multifamily dwelling
Detached ADUs: up to 1,200 sq ft, 16 feet height, 4-foot setbacks
Attached ADUs: up to 50% of existing living area or 1,200 sq ft, whichever is less
JADUs: maximum 500 sq ft, within existing structure footprint
No parking requirements for ADUs within 1/2 mile of transit
No impact fees for ADUs under 750 sq ft
60-day ministerial approval timeline
No owner-occupancy requirement
Cannot be sold separately from primary dwelling (with exceptions for affordable housing)

sections.title: Inspections Required
sections.summary: Specifies required inspections during and after construction work performed under a building permit.
key_points: Foundation inspection: after excavation and forms are set
Framing inspection: after roof, framing, fire blocking, and bracing are in place
Electrical rough-in inspection: before covering
Plumbing rough-in inspection: before covering
Mechanical rough-in inspection: before covering
Insulation inspection: after insulation is installed
Final inspection: after all work is complete
All inspections must be requested at least 24 hours in advance
Work must not be covered until inspection approval

sections.title: Plan Review Process
sections.summary: Describes the plan review process for building permits, including submittal requirements, review timelines, and corrections procedures.
key_points: Plans must be submitted with permit application
Plans reviewed for compliance with building codes, zoning, and other regulations
First review typically completed within 4-6 weeks for residential, 6-12 weeks for commercial
Corrections must be addressed and resubmitted
Plans must be prepared by licensed professionals when required by state law
Electronic plan submittal available through ProjectDox

sections.title: Building Height Regulations
sections.summary: Establishes height limits for buildings based on the applicable zone.
key_points: Height limits vary by zone from 30 feet (RS zones) to unlimited (downtown)
Measured from grade to highest point of structure
Exceptions for chimneys, antennas, mechanical equipment screening
Coastal zones may have additional height restrictions
ADUs limited to 16 feet for detached units

sections.title: Parking Regulations
sections.summary: Establishes off-street parking requirements for various land uses.
key_points: Single-family homes: 2 spaces per unit
Multi-family: varies by number of bedrooms and location
ADUs: no parking required within 1/2 mile of transit stop
Commercial: varies by use type and square footage
Parking reductions available in transit priority areas
Tandem parking allowed for ADUs and some residential uses

sections.title: Environmentally Sensitive Lands
sections.summary: Regulations for development on or near environmentally sensitive lands including wetlands, steep hillsides, and sensitive biological resources.
key_points: Development restricted on slopes greater than 25%
Wetlands and vernal pools require buffers
Biological surveys may be required
CEQA compliance required for significant impacts
Multi-Habitat Planning Area (MHPA) has specific development restrictions

sections.title: CALGreen Building Standards
sections.summary: San Diego's adoption of California Green Building Standards Code with local amendments for energy efficiency and sustainability.
key_points: All new construction must meet CALGreen Tier 1 or Tier 2 standards
Solar-ready requirements for new residential construction
Electric vehicle charging infrastructure required for new buildings
Water efficiency requirements exceed state minimums
Cool roof requirements for certain building types
